subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Absolute and Exclusive Jurisdiction — s. 553 and s. 469
fact_pattern: A client is charged with theft under $5,000 contrary to s. 322 of the Criminal Code. The client asks her lawyer which court will try the case and whether she has the right to elect her mode of trial.
call_of_question: Which court has jurisdiction to try this charge, and does the accused have the right to elect her mode of trial?

options.A: The Ontario Court of Justice has absolute jurisdiction over the charge under s. 553 of the Criminal Code; the accused has no right to elect her mode of trial.
options.B: The accused must be tried in the Superior Court of Justice because all theft charges carry a potential penalty exceeding six months.
options.C: The accused may elect to be tried by a superior court judge alone or by a superior court judge and jury.
options.D: The Ontario Court of Justice has jurisdiction only if the Crown elects to proceed summarily; if the Crown proceeds by indictment, the accused may elect her mode of trial.

explanation: Under s. 553 of the Criminal Code, certain indictable offences fall within the absolute jurisdiction of a provincial court judge (in Ontario, the Ontario Court of Justice). Theft under $5,000 (s. 322 where the value does not exceed $5,000) is listed in s. 553(a). Where an offence falls within the absolute jurisdiction of a provincial court judge, the accused has no right to elect her mode of trial — the case is tried by a provincial court judge without a jury and without a preliminary inquiry. This is distinct from hybrid offences or other indictable offences where election rights arise.
why_A_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Theft under $5,000 falls within s. 553 absolute jurisdiction; no election is available.
why_B_wrong: The potential penalty does not determine which court has absolute jurisdiction. Section 553 specifically lists theft under $5,000 as within the absolute jurisdiction of a provincial court judge regardless of the maximum penalty for theft generally.
why_C_wrong: The right to elect between superior court modes arises under s. 536 for non-s. 553 indictable offences. Theft under $5,000 falls under s. 553 absolute jurisdiction, so no election is available.
why_D_wrong: Theft under $5,000 is not a hybrid offence — the Crown's election is not relevant. The offence falls under s. 553 and must be tried by a provincial court judge. The Crown's summary/indictable election applies to hybrid offences, not s. 553 offences.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Absolute and Exclusive Jurisdiction
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 553(a)

tested_concepts: s. 553 absolute jurisdiction of provincial court judge
no election right for s. 553 offences

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Summary, Indictable, and Hybrid Offences — Crown Election
fact_pattern: Raj is charged with assault causing bodily harm, which is a hybrid offence under s. 267 of the Criminal Code. The Crown has not yet made its election. Raj's defence counsel advises him that the Crown's choice will significantly affect the available penalties and the court in which Raj will be tried.
call_of_question: Who elects the mode of proceeding for a hybrid offence, and when must that election be made?

options.A: The accused elects whether to proceed summarily or by indictment at the time of arraignment.
options.B: The Crown elects whether to proceed summarily or by indictment; it must make this election before the accused is required to plead.
options.C: The court decides whether to proceed summarily or by indictment based on the seriousness of the offence as disclosed in the information.
options.D: The accused and Crown jointly elect the mode of proceeding, and the court must approve the joint election.

explanation: For hybrid offences (also called Crown election offences or dual procedure offences), it is the Crown — not the accused — that elects whether to proceed summarily or by indictment. The Crown must make this election before the accused is required to plead to the charge. Once the Crown elects, the offence is treated as either a summary conviction or indictable offence for all subsequent purposes, including jurisdiction, limitation periods, mode of trial, and available penalties. If the Crown proceeds summarily on assault causing bodily harm, the maximum is 18 months imprisonment; by indictment, the maximum is 10 years.
why_A_wrong: The accused does not elect the mode of proceeding for hybrid offences. It is the Crown's election, not the accused's, that determines whether the matter proceeds summarily or by indictment.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The Crown elects the mode of proceeding for hybrid offences before the accused is required to plead.
why_C_wrong: The court does not decide the mode of proceeding for hybrid offences. The court's role is adjudicative; the Crown's prosecutorial discretion governs which mode is chosen.
why_D_wrong: There is no joint election mechanism for hybrid offences. The Crown alone decides whether to proceed summarily or by indictment. Court approval of the election is not required.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Summary, Indictable, and Hybrid Offences
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 267; interpretation of hybrid offences

tested_concepts: effect of election on penalties and jurisdiction

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Absolute and Exclusive Jurisdiction — s. 553 and s. 469
fact_pattern: Marcus is charged with first degree murder under s. 235 of the Criminal Code. His defence counsel explains that the case cannot be tried by a provincial court judge and that the accused's election is also limited in this case.
call_of_question: Which court has exclusive jurisdiction to try first degree murder, and what election right does the accused have?

options.A: The Ontario Court of Justice has jurisdiction if the Crown elects to proceed by indictment; the accused may elect a jury or judge-alone trial.
options.B: The Superior Court of Justice has exclusive jurisdiction under s. 469 of the Criminal Code; the accused may still elect between a judge-alone trial and a jury trial.
options.C: The Superior Court of Justice has exclusive jurisdiction under s. 469 of the Criminal Code; the accused has no right to elect — the trial must be by judge and jury unless the accused and Crown jointly apply for a judge-alone trial.
options.D: The Court of Appeal has original jurisdiction for murder charges because of their seriousness.

explanation: Under s. 469 of the Criminal Code, certain offences — including murder, treason, alarming Her Majesty, seditious offences, piracy, and others — fall within the exclusive jurisdiction of a superior court. First degree murder under s. 235 is an s. 469 offence. When an accused is charged with an s. 469 offence, there is no standard election under s. 536. The trial must proceed before a superior court judge and jury unless the accused and the Attorney General both consent and the judge approves a judge-alone trial under s. 473. This is the reverse of the usual rule: for most indictable offences, a jury trial is an option the accused chooses; for s. 469 offences, a jury trial is the default and a judge-alone trial requires consent from both sides.
why_A_wrong: The Ontario Court of Justice has no jurisdiction over s. 469 offences such as murder. First degree murder must be tried in the Superior Court of Justice regardless of the Crown's mode of election.
why_B_wrong: The accused does not have a free-standing right to elect a judge-alone trial for s. 469 offences. Under s. 473, a judge-alone trial requires the consent of both the accused and the Attorney General, plus court approval. By default, s. 469 offences are tried by judge and jury.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The Superior Court has exclusive jurisdiction under s. 469; jury trial is the default for murder; judge-alone requires joint consent under s. 473.
why_D_wrong: Courts of appeal do not have original (first instance) jurisdiction over murder. They hear appeals from trial court decisions. The Superior Court of Justice is the court of first instance for s. 469 offences.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Absolute and Exclusive Jurisdiction
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, ss. 469, 473

tested_concepts: s. 469 exclusive jurisdiction of superior court
murder requires jury trial by default
s. 473 joint consent for judge-alone on s. 469 offences

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Accused's Election — Three Modes of Trial for Indictable Offences
fact_pattern: Samira is charged with fraud over $5,000 contrary to s. 380(1)(a) of the Criminal Code. The offence is not listed in s. 553 (absolute provincial court jurisdiction) and is not an s. 469 offence. Samira asks her lawyer to explain the three options available to her for mode of trial.
call_of_question: Which statement correctly describes the three modes of trial available to Samira under s. 536 of the Criminal Code?

options.A: Samira may elect: (1) Ontario Court of Justice judge without a jury and without a preliminary inquiry; (2) Ontario Court of Justice judge without a jury but with a preliminary inquiry; (3) Superior Court judge and jury.
options.B: Samira may elect: (1) Ontario Court of Justice judge without a jury; (2) Superior Court of Justice judge without a jury; (3) Superior Court of Justice judge and jury.
options.C: Samira may elect: (1) summary conviction trial in the Ontario Court of Justice; (2) indictable trial before a superior court judge alone; (3) jury trial in the Superior Court.
options.D: Samira has no right to elect — for fraud over $5,000, only the Crown determines the mode of trial.

explanation: For indictable offences that are neither within the absolute jurisdiction of a provincial court judge (s. 553) nor within the exclusive jurisdiction of a superior court (s. 469), the accused has the right under s. 536(2) to elect the mode of trial. The three modes are: (1) trial by a provincial court judge sitting alone without a jury (Ontario Court of Justice); (2) trial by a superior court judge sitting alone without a jury (Superior Court of Justice); and (3) trial by a superior court judge sitting with a jury. Each mode has different implications for preliminary inquiries and procedural steps. Fraud over $5,000 is a non-s. 553, non-s. 469 indictable offence, so Samira has a full election under s. 536.
why_A_wrong: The second option in this answer — Ontario Court of Justice with a preliminary inquiry — does not exist as a distinct mode. Preliminary inquiries under s. 535 are held in provincial court before the accused is committed to stand trial, but they precede election; they are not a separate mode of trial alongside the three modes in s. 536.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The three modes under s. 536 are: (1) provincial court judge alone; (2) superior court judge alone; (3) superior court judge and jury.
why_C_wrong: Fraud over $5,000 is an indictable offence, not a hybrid offence. The summary conviction option is not available for indictable offences regardless of the accused's preference. The Crown would have to elect summarily for a hybrid offence, but fraud over $5,000 is not hybrid.
why_D_wrong: Fraud over $5,000 is not an s. 469 offence requiring default jury trial, and it is not an s. 553 offence of absolute provincial court jurisdiction. Accordingly, Samira has the full election right under s. 536. The Crown does not determine mode of trial for non-hybrid indictable offences.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Accused's Election — Three Modes of Trial
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 536(2)

tested_concepts: superior court judge alone vs judge and jury

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: The Crown proceeds summarily against the accused for assault (a hybrid offence). The offence allegedly occurred on January 15. Defence counsel discovers the Crown laid the information on August 10 of the same year — seven and a half months later. Defence brings an application to dismiss the information as out of time.
call_of_question: Under the Criminal Code, what is the limitation period within which an information charging a summary conviction offence must be laid?

options.A: There is no limitation period for summary conviction offences; charges may be laid at any time.
options.B: The information must be laid within 12 months of the time when the subject-matter of the proceedings arose.
options.C: The information must be laid within 6 months of the time when the subject-matter of the proceedings arose.
options.D: The information must be laid within 2 years of the time when the subject-matter of the proceedings arose.

explanation: Under s. 786(2) of the Criminal Code, no proceedings shall be instituted for a summary conviction offence more than six months after the time when the subject-matter of the proceedings arose, unless the prosecutor and the defendant consent to proceedings being instituted after that time. The six-month limitation period is one of the defining features distinguishing summary conviction proceedings from indictable proceedings, which have no limitation period. On these facts, the information was laid approximately seven and a half months after the alleged offence — beyond the six-month window — and the application to dismiss should succeed absent consent from both parties.
why_A_wrong: Summary conviction offences do carry a limitation period under s. 786(2) of the Criminal Code. The six-month limitation period is a fundamental characteristic of summary conviction procedure that must be respected.
why_B_wrong: The limitation period under s. 786(2) is six months, not twelve months. A twelve-month period does not appear in the Criminal Code's summary conviction limitation rule.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 786(2), summary conviction proceedings must be instituted within six months of when the subject-matter arose, unless both parties consent to a later institution.
why_D_wrong: A two-year limitation period does not apply to summary conviction offences under the Criminal Code. The prescribed period under s. 786(2) is six months. Two-year limitation periods appear in some provincial and federal regulatory statutes, not in the Criminal Code's summary conviction regime.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Summary Conviction Procedure — Limitation Period
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 786(2)

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: Anson is charged with conspiracy to commit murder. His lawyer advises him that conspiracy to commit murder is listed in s. 469 of the Criminal Code and that his mode of trial options are limited. Anson wishes to be tried by a superior court judge alone without a jury.
call_of_question: Under the Criminal Code, may Anson be tried by a superior court judge alone for an s. 469 offence, and if so, what is required?

options.A: No — all s. 469 offences must be tried by a superior court judge and jury; there is no mechanism to remove the jury.
options.B: Yes — Anson may unilaterally elect to be tried by judge alone by notifying the registrar of his preference before the trial commences.
options.C: Yes — under s. 473 of the Criminal Code, a judge-alone trial for an s. 469 offence may be ordered if both the accused and the Attorney General consent in writing and the court approves.
options.D: No — Anson must first complete a preliminary inquiry before any application for judge-alone trial may be made.

explanation: Under s. 473 of the Criminal Code, a superior court judge may order that a trial for an s. 469 offence proceed without a jury if both the accused and the Attorney General (or their counsel) consent in writing and the judge is satisfied it is in the interests of justice to do so. The default mode of trial for s. 469 offences is a superior court judge and jury — unlike the standard s. 536 election offences where the accused can unilaterally choose judge alone. For s. 469 offences, the jury can only be removed by the dual-consent process under s. 473.
why_A_wrong: Section 473 of the Criminal Code expressly provides a mechanism to conduct a judge-alone trial for s. 469 offences. The jury is the default, not an absolute requirement — it can be removed by the process in s. 473.
why_B_wrong: Anson cannot unilaterally elect judge alone for an s. 469 offence. Unlike s. 536 elections, which are the accused's unilateral right, the s. 473 process requires the Attorney General's consent and the court's approval.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 473 permits a judge-alone trial for s. 469 offences with the written consent of both the accused and the Attorney General and with the court's approval.
why_D_wrong: Section 473 does not require a prior preliminary inquiry as a precondition. The consent mechanism is available independently of whether a preliminary inquiry has been held or waived.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Absolute and Exclusive Jurisdiction — s. 469 and s. 473
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 469, s. 473

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Re-Election of Mode of Trial — Downgrading Election
fact_pattern: Marco initially elected to be tried by a superior court judge and jury. He has been committed to stand trial following a preliminary inquiry. He now wishes to change his election to trial by provincial court judge alone. The Crown has not consented.
call_of_question: Under s. 561 of the Criminal Code, may Marco re-elect to trial by provincial court judge alone after committal, and does he need Crown consent?

options.A: No — once an accused is committed to stand trial after a preliminary inquiry, no re-election is possible.
options.B: Yes — Marco may re-elect downward to provincial court judge alone before trial commences, without Crown consent, within the timelines prescribed in s. 561.
options.C: Yes — Marco may re-elect downward at any time before trial, but he requires Crown consent regardless of when the re-election is made.
options.D: Yes — Marco may re-elect but only with leave of a superior court judge.

explanation: Section 561 of the Criminal Code governs re-elections after committal for trial. Under s. 561(1)(a), an accused who has been committed to stand trial may re-elect the mode of trial at any time before the trial commences. Where the accused is re-electing to a lower mode — from superior court judge and jury to provincial court judge alone — Crown consent is not required provided the re-election is made within the time limits specified in s. 561. The mechanism allows a 'downward' re-election without Crown consent, though the accused must provide notice in the prescribed form.
why_A_wrong: Section 561 expressly permits re-election after committal. Committal does not freeze the accused's mode of trial choice. Section 561 specifically contemplates post-committal re-elections.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 561 permits downward re-election after committal without Crown consent, within the prescribed timelines and with appropriate notice.
why_C_wrong: Crown consent is not required for a downward re-election under s. 561 after the accused has been committed. Crown consent matters for certain upward re-elections made before the preliminary inquiry concludes.
why_D_wrong: Judicial leave is not required for a re-election under s. 561. The process involves giving notice in the prescribed form; it is not a court application requiring judicial discretion to grant leave.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Re-Election of Mode of Trial — s. 561
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 561(1)

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Section 11(b) Charter — Trial Within a Reasonable Time
fact_pattern: Vera was charged with fraud in April 2023. Her trial in a superior court was completed in November 2025 — approximately 31 months after the charge was laid. Defence counsel brought a s. 11(b) Charter application arguing the delay was unreasonable. Crown argues the case was unusually complex and that institutional delay accounts for the excess.
call_of_question: Under R. v. Jordan, 2016 SCC 27, which statement best describes how the court determines whether the delay is presumptively unreasonable?

options.A: The court conducts a four-factor balancing test, weighing the length of delay, the reason for the delay, waiver of delay, and prejudice to the accused.
options.B: Under Jordan, a ceiling of 18 months for provincial court cases and 30 months for superior court cases applies; delay beyond the ceiling is presumptively unreasonable unless exceptional circumstances justify it.
options.C: The delay is unreasonable if it exceeds 24 months in all courts regardless of the complexity of the case.
options.D: The accused must prove actual prejudice from the delay before a s. 11(b) violation can be found.

explanation: In R. v. Jordan, 2016 SCC 27, the Supreme Court replaced the Morin balancing framework with a presumptive ceiling approach. The ceiling is 18 months from charge to trial for cases tried in provincial court, and 30 months for cases requiring a preliminary inquiry or tried in superior court. Delay beyond the ceiling is presumptively unreasonable, and the Crown must rebut the presumption by establishing exceptional circumstances — such as an unforeseeable discrete event or genuinely exceptional complexity. On Vera's facts, the 31-month delay in a superior court case exceeds the 30-month ceiling, making the delay presumptively unreasonable. The Crown's complexity argument is relevant to the exceptional-circumstances analysis, not to the initial presumption.
why_A_wrong: The Morin four-factor balancing test (length, reason, waiver, prejudice) was the pre-Jordan approach. Jordan replaced that framework with presumptive ceilings, reducing reliance on the subjective prejudice factor and creating clearer rules.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Jordan establishes presumptive ceilings of 18 months (provincial court) and 30 months (superior court); delay beyond these is presumptively unreasonable absent exceptional circumstances.
why_C_wrong: Jordan does not impose a single 24-month ceiling for all courts. The ceilings differ: 18 months for provincial court and 30 months for superior court. A uniform 24-month rule would misstate the Jordan framework.
why_D_wrong: Jordan explicitly rejected the requirement that the accused prove actual prejudice. Delay beyond the presumptive ceiling is itself presumptively unreasonable; actual prejudice is no longer a necessary element of a s. 11(b) violation under the Jordan framework.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Section 11(b) — Trial Within a Reasonable Time
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 11(b); R. v. Jordan, 2016 SCC 27

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Consequences of Crown Election on Penalties — 2019 Amendment
fact_pattern: Damaris is charged with uttering threats under s. 264.1(1) of the Criminal Code, a hybrid offence. The Crown has elected to proceed summarily. Defence counsel explains to Damaris the maximum penalty she now faces as a result of the Crown election.
call_of_question: Under s. 787(1) of the Criminal Code as currently in force, what is the default maximum penalty for a summary conviction offence that does not specify a different maximum?

options.A: A fine of $5,000 only; no custodial sentence is available on summary conviction.
options.B: Six months imprisonment and/or a fine of $5,000, reflecting the historical default.
options.C: Two years less a day imprisonment and/or a fine of $5,000.
options.D: Five years imprisonment, which is the standard maximum for all Criminal Code hybrid offences.

explanation: Under s. 787(1) of the Criminal Code, as amended by the Criminal Code and Other Acts (Miscellaneous Amendments) Act, S.C. 2019, c. 25, the default maximum punishment for a summary conviction offence (unless a different punishment is specifically provided) is a fine of not more than $5,000 or a term of imprisonment of not more than two years less a day, or both. This amendment came into force on September 19, 2019, and significantly increased the default summary conviction maximum from the former six-month ceiling. Defence counsel must advise clients about this current maximum, not the former six-month rule.
why_A_wrong: Custodial sentences are available on summary conviction under s. 787(1). The provision expressly permits imprisonment of up to two years less a day. The fine-only characterization of summary conviction is incorrect.
why_B_wrong: The six-month maximum was the former default under s. 787(1) prior to the 2019 Criminal Code amendments. Since September 2019, the default maximum is two years less a day. The six-month figure is outdated for offences without a specific summary conviction penalty.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 787(1) as amended in 2019, the default maximum for summary conviction offences is two years less a day imprisonment and/or a fine of $5,000.
why_D_wrong: Five years is not the summary conviction maximum under s. 787(1). Some hybrid offences specify a higher indictable maximum, but the default summary conviction maximum is two years less a day. The five-year figure relates to certain specific indictable maxima or the threshold for s. 11(f) jury trial rights.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Summary Conviction — Maximum Penalty
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 787(1) (as amended S.C. 2019, c. 25)
